perturbation.original_text:
Throughout the Term of this Agreement, M&I shall maintain at all times at its own cost and expense:
1. Commercial General Liability Insurance covering its premises, including bodily injury, property damage, broad form contractual liability and independent contractors, with primary limits of not less than two million dollars ($2,000,000).
2. Fidelity Insurance covering employee dishonesty with respect to all aspects of the Services, in an amount not less than ten million dollars ($10,000,000).
3. Workers' Compensation Insurance as mandated or allowed by the state in which the Services are being performed, including at least five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) coverage for Employer's Liability.
4. All Risk Property Insurance in an amount adequate to cover the cost of replacement of all equipment, improvements, and betterments at M&I locations in the event of loss or damage.
perturbation.changed_text:
Throughout the Term of this Agreement, M&I shall maintain at all times at its own cost and expense:
1. Commercial General Liability Insurance covering its premises.
2. Fidelity Insurance covering employee dishonesty with respect to all aspects of the Services.
3. Workers' Compensation Insurance as mandated or allowed by the state in which the Services are being performed.
4. Property Insurance in an amount determined by M&I.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific coverage details and monetary limits on the insurance policies M&I is required to maintain, the contract creates a legal risk.  Wisconsin law mandates adequate insurance coverage to protect against potential liabilities. Omitting specific coverage amounts could lead to disputes over the adequacy of the coverage and expose Tri City National Bank to financial risks if M&I's insurance proves insufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute § 895.46(1)(a) generally requires the state to ensure sufficient insurance coverage is in place to protect against liabilities. By removing the required minimum coverage and specific policy attributes, the amended text does not ensure adequate liability coverage, potentially violating this state statute and leaving Tri City National Bank at risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wisconsin Statutes § 895.46 (2022) — State and political subdivisions thereof to pay judgments taken against officers. :: 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2023 Wis. Stat. & Ann. 2022 Wis. Stat. & Ann. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Wisconsin Statutes & Annotations Chapter 895 - Damages, liability, and miscellaneous provisions regarding actions in courts. 895.46 - State and political subdivisions thereof to pay judgments taken against officers. Universal Citation: WI Stat § 895.46 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 895.46 State and political subdivisions thereof to pay judgments taken against officers. (1) (a) If the defendant in any action or special proceeding is a public officer or employee and is proceeded against in an official capacity or is proceeded against as an individual because of acts committed while carrying out duties as an officer or employee and the jury or the court finds that the defendant was acting within the scope of employment, the judgment as to damages and costs entered against the officer or employee, except as provided in s. 146.89 (4), in excess of any insurance applicable to the officer or employee shall be paid by the state or political subdivision of which the defendant is an officer or employee. Agents of any department of the state shall be covered by this section while acting within the scope of their agency. Regardless of the results of the litigation the governmental unit, if it does not provide legal counsel to the defendant officer or employee, shall pay reasonable attorney fees and costs of defending the action, unless it is found by the court or jury that the defendant officer or employee did not act within the scope of employment. Except as provided in s. 146.89
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes specific coverage details and monetary limits on the insurance policies that M&I is required to maintain. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this creates a legal risk, particularly concerning adequate insurance coverage under Wisconsin law. The Law Explanation states that Wisconsin Statute § 895.46(1)(a) generally requires the state to ensure sufficient insurance coverage is in place to protect against liabilities, and by removing the required minimum coverage and specific policy attributes, the amended text potentially violates this statute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports the Law Explanation, stating in 895.46 (1) (a) that if a public officer or employee is acting within the scope of employment, the judgment as to damages and costs entered against the officer or employee in excess of any insurance applicable to the officer or employee shall be paid by the state or political subdivision of which the defendant is an officer or employee. The omission of specific insurance coverage amounts increases the risk that liabilities may not be adequately covered, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: M&I shall comply with, and M&I shall provide Customer with data and reports necessary for Customer to comply with, all federal laws applicable to the transactions or accounts processed by M&I. Customer shall have the right to notify M&I of any requirements or changes in state law which affect the provision of the Services. Thereafter, M&I shall schedule implementation of the changes prior to the deadline imposed by the regulatory or other governmental agency having jurisdiction for such change. M&I's obligation to meet the compliance deadline shall be contingent upon M&I receiving timely notice from Customer or any other service bureau customer of M&I so as to enable M&I to schedule and implement such change prior to the regulatory deadline. M&I shall implement such change at Customer's sole cost and expense (shared equitably among all of M&I's other service bureau customers who are affected by such change).
perturbation.changed_text: M&I shall comply with all federal laws applicable to the transactions or accounts processed by M&I. Customer shall have the right to notify M&I of any requirements or changes in state law which affect the provision of the Services. M&I shall implement such change at Customer's sole cost and expense.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause mandating M&I to provide data and reports necessary for the customer to comply with federal laws and the clause regarding the implementation of changes to comply with state law by a regulatory deadline, the contract now contradicts regulatory compliance standards. Banks are legally obligated to adhere to both federal and state regulations, and this change reduces M&I's responsibility to aid in this compliance. This creates legal risks for Tri City National Bank, particularly concerning reporting requirements and adherence to state-specific banking laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 12 U.S.C. § 1818(i) pertains to the enforcement of regulations and orders, highlighting the importance of banks complying with regulatory mandates. By omitting M&I's responsibility to provide necessary data and meet compliance deadlines, the modified text weakens Tri City National Bank's ability to adhere to federal regulations, thereby increasing the risk of non-compliance and potential penalties as outlined in this US Code section.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates M&I to furnish data and reports for Customer compliance with federal laws and implement changes for state law compliance by regulatory deadlines. The changed text omits these obligations. The LLM explanation states that 12 U.S.C. § 1818(i) pertains to the enforcement of regulations, highlighting the importance of banks complying with regulatory mandates. By removing M&I's responsibility to provide necessary data and meet compliance deadlines, the modified text weakens Tri City National Bank's ability to adhere to federal regulations. The 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant. Here, M&I's omission of responsibilities related to data provision and meeting compliance deadlines creates non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The responsibility and expense for transportation and transmission of, and the risk of loss for, data and media transmitted between M&I and Customer shall be borne by Customer. Data lost by M&I following processing, including loss of data transmission, shall either be restored by M&I from its back-up media or shall be reprocessed at no charge.
perturbation.changed_text: The responsibility and expense for transportation and transmission of, and the risk of loss for, data and media transmitted between M&I and Customer shall be borne by Customer.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that mandates M&I to restore data lost after processing or reprocess it at no charge, the contract creates a legal loophole concerning data security and liability. Federal regulations, such as those related to data protection and privacy, require financial institutions to ensure the integrity and availability of customer data.  This omission creates a risk for Tri City National Bank as it places the entire burden of data loss on the Customer, contradicting regulations concerning data handling responsibilities of financial service providers.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 6801, part of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, requires financial institutions to protect the security and confidentiality of customers' nonpublic personal information. By removing the obligation for M&I to restore or reprocess lost data, the altered contract fails to ensure the protection of this information as required by this law, making the bank potentially non-compliant.

perturbation.justification: The omission of M&I's obligation to restore or reprocess lost data directly contradicts the principle of protecting customer's nonpublic information, as emphasized in 15 U.S.C. § 6801. The original contract ensured data integrity through restoration or reprocessing at no charge, while the changed contract shifts the entire burden of data loss to the customer, undermining the financial institution's responsibility for data security. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that each financial institution has an "affirmative and continuing obligation to respect the privacy of its customers and to protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information". By removing the clause, the bank potentially violates the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act because the contract no longer ensures the protection of customer data that gets lost after processing. This also aligns with Scraped Law Snippet 2, which emphasizes maintaining "safeguards to protect the security of customer information."
